lessons.title: std::ranges::to — Materializing Ranges into Containers

background.title: View adaptors are lazy (C++20)
background.body: In C++20, view adaptors (filter, transform, take, etc.) are lazy: they store no elements and compute them on demand during iteration. This means a pipeline of views is not a vector — it is a description of a computation. You cannot pass such a view directly to an API that expects a vector; explicit materialization is required.

lessons.motivation: The ranges library introduced in C++20 brought powerful tools for composing algorithms via view pipelines. However, turning a finished pipeline back into a regular container was awkward: you had to construct a vector via iterators, or use std::ranges::copy with a back_inserter. For complex pipelines this became boilerplate that obscured the intent. C++23 introduced std::ranges::to — a single, uniform way to materialize any range into any container in one line.
lessons.theory:
Before C++23, two approaches existed for converting a range into a container. The first was construction via an iterator pair: std::vector<int> result(filtered.begin(), filtered.end()). The second was std::ranges::copy(filtered, std::back_inserter(result)). Both are verbose, require explicitly naming the element type, and do not compose cleanly in a pipe chain.

std::ranges::to<C>() solves this. It supports two syntaxes: pipe syntax (range | std::ranges::to<std::vector>()) and function call syntax (std::ranges::to<std::vector>(range)). With CTAD the element type is deduced automatically — std::ranges::to<std::vector>() without explicit element type angle brackets. When a specific element type is needed it can be given explicitly: std::ranges::to<std::vector<int>>().

The function works with any container that has a constructor taking std::from_range_t (added in C++23 for vector, list, deque, set, map, and other standard containers), or that supports push_back or insert. This means std::ranges::to also works with user-defined containers that meet these requirements. Conversion between container types is natural: a list can become a set (with automatic deduplication and sorting), a set can become a vector, and a range of characters can become a string.

For nested ranges std::ranges::to works recursively: a range of ranges can be collected into a vector<vector<...>>, with the inner to applied to each nested range inside a transform step. This makes std::ranges::to a universal terminal step for any pipeline, regardless of its depth or complexity.

examples.title: Filter + transform → vector
examples.description: A filter-and-transform pipeline is materialized into a vector with a single std::ranges::to call. CTAD deduces the element type automatically.

examples.title: Converting between container types and to string
examples.description: std::ranges::to makes container conversions natural: vector to set for deduplication, set back to vector for a sorted sequence, and a range of chars into a string.

examples.title: Nested ranges: vector<vector<string>>
examples.description: When working with nested ranges, std::ranges::to is applied at each level. Here each sentence is split by spaces into a vector of words, and the results are collected into an outer vector.

lessons.title: Splitting Adapters: chunk, slide, stride, chunk_by
lessons.motivation: After learning to materialize ranges into containers, the next question is how to split ranges into parts. Batch processing, sliding windows for statistics, signal downsampling, grouping consecutive elements — these are common tasks that in C++20 required manual index loops or awkward algorithms. C++23 adds four specialized adapters: views::chunk, views::slide, views::stride, and views::chunk_by — each solving its own class of splitting problem in a single line.
lessons.theory:
views::chunk(n) splits a range into consecutive, non-overlapping blocks of n elements. Each block is itself a view. If the range size is not divisible by n, the last block contains the remaining elements — fewer than n. This directly replaces a manual loop with step n: batch API requests, paginated record processing, splitting a byte stream into fixed-size packets.

views::slide(n) also produces windows of size n, but adjacent windows overlap: each window is offset by one element from the previous. For the range [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], slide(3) yields [1,2,3], [2,3,4], [3,4,5]. The number of windows equals max(0, size - n + 1). This is the standard tool for moving averages, rolling maxima, and any other windowed statistic.

views::stride(n) does not create windows — it picks every nth element: the first, then the (n+1)th, the (2n+1)th, and so on. For [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], stride(2) gives [1, 3, 5]. Use cases include downsampling signals, accessing interleaved data, and selecting every kth frame from a video stream.

views::chunk_by(pred) splits a range into variable-size groups via a predicate: while pred(prev, cur) returns true, elements stay in the same group; when it returns false, a new group begins. Unlike chunk and slide, group sizes are not fixed. This is well suited for run-length encoding, grouping consecutive equal elements, and splitting a partially sorted range. A brief comparison: chunk — fixed size, no overlap; slide — fixed size, overlapping (step 1); stride — picks single elements at a regular interval; chunk_by — variable size, controlled by a predicate on adjacent pairs.

examples.title: chunk and stride side by side
examples.description: chunk(3) slices a vector into triples (the last may be shorter). stride(3) picks every third element. Both adapters are lazy views; results are materialized via ranges::to.

examples.title: slide for moving average and chunk_by for grouping
examples.description: slide(3) produces overlapping windows over which an average is computed. chunk_by(<=) groups adjacent elements while each successor is not less than its predecessor, isolating non-decreasing runs.

examples.title: Practical: batch processing and downsampling
examples.description: chunk(3) simulates batched API calls — the last batch is partial. stride(2) downsamples a sensor reading array by half, keeping every other value.

lessons.title: Combining Adapters: zip, enumerate, cartesian_product, join_with
lessons.motivation: The previous lessons showed how to split ranges into parts. An equally important question is the reverse: how do you iterate multiple ranges together, or combine them in various ways? Iterating two vectors in parallel used to require a manual index loop. Indexed traversal meant a counter variable next to a range-for. All combinations of two sets meant nested loops. Inserting a delimiter between substrings meant accumulation with a conditional. C++23 adds a set of adapters that handle all these tasks declaratively: views::zip, views::enumerate, views::cartesian_product, views::join_with, and views::adjacent.
lessons.theory:
views::zip(r1, r2, ...) traverses multiple ranges in lockstep, yielding a tuple of corresponding elements on each step. Iteration stops when the shortest range is exhausted. Structured bindings work naturally: auto [a, b] = *it. Before C++23 this required a manual index loop or Boost.Range. The related adapter views::zip_transform(f, r1, r2) does the same but immediately applies a function f to each tuple — convenient for element-wise arithmetic.

views::enumerate(r) numbers the elements of a range: each iteration yields a pair (index, element), where the index starts at 0 and has a signed integer type (ptrdiff_t). Structured bindings auto [i, val] replace the classic counter next to a range-for. This is the C++ equivalent of Python's enumerate(), absent from the language until C++23.

views::cartesian_product(r1, r2, ...) generates all combinations of elements from multiple ranges — like nested for-loops. The rightmost range varies fastest (inner loop), the leftmost slowest (outer loop). The total number of elements equals the product of all range sizes. Common uses include enumerating grid coordinates, parameter sweeps, and combinatorics problems.

views::join_with(delim) concatenates a range-of-ranges, inserting a delimiter between the inner ranges. Unlike views::join from C++20, which simply concatenates, join_with lets you specify delim — a single element or a range of elements. This allows joining strings with a separator without creating intermediate buffers. When the delimiter is a string, pass it as a view (via std::views::all or string_view) because join_with expects a range of characters, not a std::string by value.

views::adjacent<N> yields overlapping tuples of N consecutive elements. views::adjacent<2> (also known as views::pairwise) gives pairs: for [1, 2, 3, 4] those are (1,2), (2,3), (3,4). Unlike slide(N), which produces subranges, adjacent yields fixed-size structured tuples of size N — making them convenient with structured bindings. Typical uses: computing differences between consecutive elements, detecting monotonic runs, processing sliding pairs.

examples.title: zip and enumerate
examples.description: views::zip iterates two vectors in lockstep — names and scores. views::enumerate adds a sequential index to each element. Structured bindings keep the code readable.
examples.expectedOutput:
Scores:
  Alice: 95
  Bob: 87
  Carol: 92
Ranked:
  1. Alice
  2. Bob
  3. Carol

examples.title: cartesian_product and zip_transform
examples.description: views::cartesian_product enumerates all (row, col) pairs — the right range varies fastest. views::zip_transform applies std::plus element-wise to two vectors.

examples.title: join_with for string joining and adjacent<2> for differences
examples.description: views::join_with inserts the separator ", " between words — the delimiter is passed as a view via std::views::all. views::adjacent<2> yields overlapping pairs for computing differences between consecutive elements.

lessons.title: Numeric Algorithms: fold_left, fold_right, repeat, ranges::contains
lessons.motivation: The previous lessons equipped us with powerful tools for splitting and combining ranges. Now we turn to algorithms that reduce a range to a single value or inspect its contents. Before C++23, summing a collection meant std::accumulate from <numeric> — which did not work directly with views and required explicit iterators. Checking for membership required an awkward comparison with ranges::find. Repeating sequences had to be constructed by hand. C++23 closes all these gaps: fold_left and fold_right replace accumulate and work with any range, views::repeat generates repeated sequences, and ranges::contains makes membership testing a one-liner.
lessons.theory:
std::ranges::fold_left(range, init, f) performs a left fold: it applies a binary function f left-to-right, accumulating the result. For a range {e1, e2, e3} with initial value init it computes f(f(f(init, e1), e2), e3). Unlike std::accumulate, fold_left works with any range including lazy views — no explicit iterators required. The function lives in <algorithm>. The variant fold_left_first(range, f) uses the first element as the initial value and returns std::optional<T> — empty if the range is empty. This is convenient when no sensible neutral element exists, such as when searching for a maximum via a lambda. Additional variants fold_left_with_iter and fold_left_first_with_iter also return the past-the-end iterator — useful when you need to know where the fold stopped.

std::ranges::fold_right(range, init, f) performs a right fold — it traverses the range from right to left. For {e1, e2, e3} it computes f(e1, f(e2, f(e3, init))). This requires a bidirectional range. For associative operations (addition, multiplication) the result matches fold_left, but note that the argument order of f is reversed: in fold_left f receives (accumulator, element), in fold_right it receives (element, accumulator). Right fold is used where evaluation order matters, such as building a string from right to left.

std::views::repeat(val) creates an infinite range of the constant value val. It must be combined with take or zip, since an infinite view cannot be materialized directly. The bounded form std::views::repeat(val, n) generates exactly n copies of val and can be piped straight into ranges::to. std::views::iota(start, end) lazily generates the arithmetic sequence [start, end) — a direct replacement for a manual counter when working with range algorithms. The unbounded iota(start) is used together with take.

std::ranges::contains(range, value) returns bool — true if value appears in range. This is a readable replacement for ranges::find(range, value) != ranges::end(range). Related algorithms: std::ranges::starts_with(range, prefix) checks whether range begins with the sub-sequence prefix; std::ranges::ends_with(range, suffix) checks the suffix. All three are in <algorithm> and work with any forward range.

examples.title: fold_left and fold_left_first: replacing accumulate
examples.description: fold_left sums integers and concatenates strings via std::plus. fold_left_first takes the first element as its initial value and returns optional — convenient for finding the maximum without a neutral element.

examples.title: fold_right, views::repeat, and views::iota
examples.description: fold_right assembles a string from right to left — notice that f's argument order is swapped compared to fold_left. views::repeat(5, 4) produces 4 copies of 5. views::iota(1, 6) generates a sequence for transform.

examples.title: ranges::contains, starts_with, ends_with, and zip
examples.description: ranges::contains checks membership without comparing against end(). starts_with and ends_with test prefix and suffix sub-sequences. The final example uses zip to pair labels with values.
examples.expectedOutput:
contains 3: true
contains 9: false
starts_with [1,2]: true
ends_with [4,5]: true
a: 10
b: 20
c: 30
